edge: One of the key concepts in the context related to the training of neural captioners is the use of human-generated references as a basis for training.
This relationship highlights a problem that arises with neural captioners, which is the generation of vague captions when not optimized for a specific communication goal.

The system described in the context is required to produce a description for a given target image in order to enable an out-of-the-box text-conditioned image retriever to identify the image among candidates.
The system is designed to produce descriptions that assist an out-of-the-box image retriever in identifying target images among a set of candidates.
The process of fine-tuning a captioner with a discriminative communication objective is discussed in the context as a means to improve the language and informativeness of captions.
The descriptions generated by the system after fine-tuning are shown to resemble human references more closely than those produced by the same system without fine-tuning.
The discriminatively fine-tuned captions are found to be more helpful than vanilla ClipCap captions or ground-truth captions for human annotators tasked with a specific dataset.
ClipCap captions are compared to ground-truth captions for human annotators in an image discrimination task. ClipCap captions are found to be more helpful than ground-truth captions.
Image descriptions are automatically generated using deep neural networks. Deep neural networks have shown impressive progress in generating image descriptions over the last decade.

Image descriptions are generated using a discriminative finetuning method applied to CLIP encoders. The CLIP encoders are frozen while the language generation modules are updated based on reward values.

System-produced captions are optimized to be similar to ground-truth human references through cross-entropy cost function or NLG metrics like CIDEr. The similarity is achieved through reward-based objectives.

An image description is produced to correctly characterize an object so that a hearer can discriminate it from other contextual elements. The ability to discriminate objects plays a core role in communication.
Communication has the core purpose of discriminating between referents.
Communication plays a fundamental role in the evolution of communication.
Communication plays a fundamental role in the acquisition of communication.

Our study builds on the earlier work of Dai and Lin, Luo et al., and Liu et al. in developing a system for discriminative finetuning and caption learning.

The ClipCap architecture uses a multimodal language model as a neural captioner.

Concerning examples (c) and (f), the human captions report that they are 'digital art' and an 'avatar', respectively, leading the annotators to pick other potential exemplars of digital art and avatars from the distractors.
The DiscriTune caption for (f) is actually inaccurate, as the avatar is not a bird, but the mention of a pink nose nevertheless helped the subject identifying the right image.
The human caption of example (d) refers to an outdoor amphitheater that is not visible in the picture.
Again, DiscriTune was more helpful to the human discriminators by providing a plain description of the picture contents.
